name: JuliaCall (Julia-Python Bridge)

performance.notes: Julia startup is slow (5-30s first call); subsequent calls near-native speed. Use persistent Julia process.

agent_readiness.notes: Call Julia from Python via PythonCall.jl. pip install juliacall. from juliacall import Main as jl. jl.seval('using LinearAlgebra'). result = jl.norm([3, 4]). → 5.0. jl.seval('f(x) = x^2 + 1'). jl.f(5). Arrays: jl.Array[jl.Float64]([1,2,3]). Pkg: jl.Pkg.add('Plots'). Type conversion: Python lists → Julia Vector, numpy arrays → Julia Array (zero-copy). jl.seval for arbitrary Julia code. juliapkg.json for dependency management. JULIA_NUM_THREADS for parallelism. Bidirectional: PythonCall.jl allows Julia to call Python too. Companion: PyJulia (older, less maintained).

gotchas: Julia startup time is significant (5-30s) — not suitable for short-lived processes
Julia must be installed separately; juliacall auto-installs a private Julia via juliaup
Type conversion edge cases: complex types may not convert automatically
juliapkg.json needed for project-level dependency management
Older PyJulia package has different API — don't confuse with juliacall
best_when: Using Julia's scientific computing ecosystem (DifferentialEquations.jl, Flux.jl) from Python
High-performance numerical computing that Python/NumPy can't match
Long-running Python services that call Julia for compute-intensive tasks
When zero-copy array sharing between Python and Julia is needed
avoid_when: Production environments without Julia installed (adds complexity)
summary: JuliaCall bridges Python and Julia. from juliacall import Main as jl; jl.seval('using Pkg'); result = jl.sqrt(2.0). Zero-copy numpy↔Julia array sharing. Julia must be installed (juliacall auto-installs via juliaup). MIT by JuliaPy. Slow startup (5-30s). Best for calling Julia's scientific stack (DifferentialEquations, Flux) from Python. Not for short-lived processes.
